---
title: "ICP and Herniation Management" 
category: "Neurocritical Care"
date-modified: today
author: Luis Silva 
source: "UF Neurology Resident Handbook 2026-2027"
---



# Acute elevations in ICP and management of elevated ICP:

## Sx of elevated ICP include:

### Non-focal:

-   Agitation
-   Headache
-   Diminished level of consciousness
-   N/V
-   hiccups
-   increased yawning
-   Cushing's response (elevated BP, bradycardia, respiratory depression)

## If any concern for worsening or impending herniation:

Clinical suspicion of sustained (\>5 min) or elevation of ICP \> 22 mmHg - Neurocritical care fellow: 352-745-1912 - Ensure appropriate vascular access, consider need for additional PIV and/or stat central line, transfer to ICU if on floor/IMC

# Herniation syndromes

[![](images/neurocritical_care/herniation_syndromes.png){width="683"}](https://mdsearchlight.com/neurology/brain-herniation/)

| Syndrome                     | Findings                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                         |
|------------------------------------|------------------------------------|
| Subfalcine                   | \- Infarct in distal ACA territory → ipsilateral cingulate gyrus migrates under anterior falx<br>- → ipsilateral leg weakness                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                    |
| Central                      | \- Ascending: compression of PCA and SCA against tentorium<br>- Descending: progression of abnormal flexor posturing to extensor posturing → impending death due to damaged respiratory and cardiac centers in medulla by herniated cerebellar tonsils through foramen magnum                                                                                                                                                                                    |
| Cerebellar tonsillar         | \- PICA, vertebral artery, anterior spinal artery infarcts → infarct to brainstem, tonsils, and lower cerebellum, upper cervical cord → respiratory arrest, downbeat nystagmus                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                   |
| Uncal                        | \- Compression of 3rd nerve against tentorial edge → constriction followed by ipsilateral pupillary dilation<br>- Compression of calcarine branch of PCA → infarct in temporal or occipital lobe<br>- Compression of sylvian aqueduct → obstructive hydrocephalus<br>- Midbrain displacement can cause compression on contralateral cerebral peduncle / contralateral corticospinal tract → ipsilateral hemiparesis to compression (Kernohan's notch phenomenon) |
| Transalar (trans-sphenoidal) | \- Descending / posterior variant: infarct in MCA territory from compression within sphenoid ridge<br>- Ascending / anterior variant: infarct in anterior and middle cerebral arteries from compression of supra-clinoid segment of ICA against anterior clinoid process                                                                                                                                                                                         |
| Transcalvarial               | \- Often after decompressive hemicraniectomy → compression of cortical vessels along bony margin predisposing to hemorrhagic infarct                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                             |

# Management of herniation

| Tier              | Management                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                             |
|------------------------------------|------------------------------------|
| Tier 0 (Standard) | \- Assess ABCs<br>- Minimize noxious stimuli (treat subclinical seizures, glucose control, treat shivering)<br>- Analgesia / sedation (control pain and agitation at the lowest effective doses (fentanyl, lorazepam, propofol)<br>- Target normothermia<br>- Head of bed ≥ 30° (unless unstable MAP or brain sag)<br>- Neck midline (optimize jugular venous drainage)<br>- Iso or hyperosmotic fluids (NO D5W, ½ NS, etc)<br>- Avoid and correct hyponatremia<br>- Steroids in select conditions (brain tumor, abscess, non-infectious neuroinflammatory conditions) |
| Tier 1            | \- Hyperosmolar therapy<br>- Temporary hyperventilation to a PaCO2 of 30-35 mmHg, consider 10 cc 1% lidocaine into ETT prior to suctioning<br>- CSF drainage<br>- Consider surgical decompression                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                      |
| Tier 2            | \- Hyperosmolar therapy for higher Na goals<br>- Optimize sedation and analgesia<br>- Rescue decompressive surgery should be considered as a life-saving intervention                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                  |
| Tier 3            | \- Sedation titrated to ICP goal or burst suppression of cEEG (pentobarbital coma)<br>- Hyperventilation to achieve mild to moderate hypocapnia (PaCO2 25-34 mmHg), short lived, \<24 hours, works by ↑ vasoconstriction and ↓ cerebral blood volume. Effects at the expense of ↓ CBF therefore used only as bridge to urgent surgical therapy. Not recommended for routine management. Can be bridge to OR in actively herniating patients<br>- Moderate hypothermia (target core temperature 32-34°C)                                                                |

# Hyperosmolar therapy

| Therapy | Dosing | Adverse Reactions | Pearls |
|---|---|---|---|
| Mannitol (0.5-1 g/kg over 5-15 mins) | - Give 20% 1g/kg bolus; max 150g<br>- Can be re-dosed every 4-6 hrs, consider re-dose if:<br>&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;- Posm <320 and ICP still >20<br>&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;- Posm >320, calculate osmolar gap (measured Posm − [2 x plasma Na + Glc/18 + BUN/2.8]); if < 20 AND Na <160, give addition 1 g/kg<br>- Repeat until ICP <20 or definitive therapy | - Rebound ICP elevation with abrupt discontinuation / repeat dosing<br>- Acute kidney injury<br>- Hypovolemia<br>- Hypotension<br>- Electrolyte imbalances | - Relative contraindications: renal failure/insufficiency<br>- May be given via peripheral<br>- Duration of effect 90 min – 6 hrs<br>- Monitor trough osmolar gap (goal <20 mOsm/kg)<br>- Maintain euvolemia – replete urine output cc for cc with normal saline |
| Hypertonic Saline | **Goals**<br>- Target Na (q2-4h): 145-155 mmol/L<br>- Target osmolarity (q4-12h): 300-320 mOsmol/L or higher as indicated<br><br>**Bolus dosing**<br>- 2% or 3%: 250 ml over 30 min<br>- 23.4%: 30 ml over 30 min q6h<br><br>**2% or 3% to maintain Na<155 or Posm<320**<br>- Na should not rise >8-10 pts in 24 hrs<br>- Normal saline (154 mmol/L)<br>&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;- Baseline hyponatremia (Na<131 mmol/L)<br>- 2% gtt (342 mmol/L): monitor Na q2-4h<br>&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;- Mild-moderate edema or baseline sodium close to target (~140 and above; patient should NOT be hyponatremic (<130))<br>&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;- Peripheral or central line<br>&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;- Initiation: 0.5-1 ml/kg/hr<br>&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;- Infusion rate not exceed 125 ml/hr<br><br>**3% gtt (513 mmol/L): monitor Na q2-4h**<br>- Severe edema or baseline sodium far from target (131-140)<br>- Central line<br>- Initiation: 0.5 ml/kg/hr<br>- Peripheral up to 48 hours at rate <50 cc/hr – discuss with NCC first<br>- Rebound edema can occur on d/c, observe in ICU ~24 hours<br>- Discontinuation: Decrease rate by 50% every 4-6 hours, once rate is 20 ml/hr, it can be stopped | - Pulmonary edema<br>- Heart failure<br>- Acute kidney injury<br>- Coagulopathy<br>- Hypernatremia<br>- Metabolic acidosis<br>- Thrombophlebitis<br>- Osmotic demyelination syndrome with rapid correction | - Central access needed for >2% gtt or 23.4% bolus<br>- Duration of effect 90 min–4 h<br>- Avoid prolonged hypernatremia > 160<br>- Decrease chloride and increase acetate content if patients develop metabolic acidosis<br>- Can be prepared as a 1:1 NaCl:NaAcetate solution if hyperchloremic metabolic acidosis<br>- If persistent hypotension with bradycardia after bolus → norepi gtt<br>- Have nurse repeat all Na samples with dramatic changes as they frequently are inaccurate<br>- If too quick drop during discontinuation, restart previous solution at prior rate with/without a 25 – 50% decrease |
